The company trades well above most of the competitors in that table on a sales basis, and it’s an even more dramatic multiple on an EBITDA basis. I can understand the premiums because of LASR’s investment phase, and its EBITDA is mostly non-existent for now, but we can also see that the market is pricing the directed-energy opportunity very aggressively.

12x sales means we’re valuing nLIGHT like a defense firm. It might be on its way to becoming one, but it’s still far off at this stage. We’ve seen that directed-energy systems are gaining serious traction within defense procurement programs, particularly as militaries search for cost-effective solutions to counter drones and other asymmetric threats. That tailwind is going to exist for the foreseeable future, but today’s price also comes with a very specific set of assumptions around it.

The first is that investors are effectively assuming that aerospace and defense will continue expanding rapidly as a share of revenue. That trend is well underway, and it is pushing the company further away from the cyclicality of the industrial laser market.

The market is also pricing in substantial margin expansion over time. The current EV/EBITDA multiple is extremely high, mostly because profitability has not yet caught up with the revenue ramp. The multiple will compress naturally, but that compression will happen when LASR’s operating leverage materializes.

Remember, too, that the guidance for Q1 FY26 is $70-76 million, and the midpoint is $73 million. If we annualize that, it comes out to ~$300 million in revenue for the year, with expected gross margins topping out at 32%. On a sales basis, the stock could be trading at a $3.6 billion market cap, not too far off today’s value. However, recent developments, like the company’s new laser weapon system, add another string to its bow, and the current conflict in Iran and the Middle East means that the company will bring in much more revenue than expected in H1 26, and likely for the full fiscal year, as weapons systems need to be expended and replaced. But that’s a topic for another forum. The takeaway here is that warfare means more money, even though it’s not always that simple.

If we assume that revenue can cross the $300 million mark and touch $340–360 million in FY26, largely because of the heightened tensions around sovereignty and defense for the rest of the year, the valuation easily touches $4.3 billion on a sales basis. That’s a 23% upside from today’s market cap of $3.5 billion, and I daresay I’m almost being too conservative. But I’m unwilling to stretch my revenue estimate any further than that, so that’s what I’ll go with. It’s still a significant jump, and a lot needs to happen for it to materialize.

At this stage, I’m calling another Buy. Yes, the price means that investors think that the pivot has already succeeded, which is true, but there is also more upside to be had, given the company’s guidance for the coming years and tailwinds from its main growth industries.

The Risks, And What Comes Next

nLIGHT’s biggest opportunity is its biggest weakness, in my opinion, and that is the A&D shift. Defense program timing is crucial to its business model now. Yes, directed-energy systems have been discussed for years, but moving from development contracts to large production deployments can take time. You must also factor in budget cycles, procurement delays, and testing timelines, because they all affect how quickly these technologies make it into operational systems. So, if that transition happens more slowly than investors expect, revenue growth will look uneven from year to year, and that’s a pattern we’ve already seen in previous years.

Then there’s customer concentration. The company’s top 10 customers brought in 75% of its revenue in FY25, and the US government is in that group, so it has become a much larger contributor to overall revenue. Obviously, that relationship is a strength in many ways, but it also means the company is exposed to any shifts in defense spending priorities or delays in contract funding.

I’d say that the next phase of the story is fairly straightforward. I want to see how the new programs move from development contracts to larger production orders because that will increase LASR’s revenue base and margin structure.

Conclusion

Looking back at the past year, my original thesis around nLIGHT has played out much better than I expected. The company has successfully shifted its major revenue source while increasing growth, and its margins are recovering nicely. However, the investment setup today looks very different from the one that existed when the stock was still being valued like a struggling industrial photonics supplier.

After such a big price rally, I don’t think that the story is finished, but the margin of safety is much smaller. Still, I’m willing to place another Buy on the stock while waiting for more validation from the fundamentals.

data.title: Texas Instruments: Recurring Efficient Capital Allocation But Limited Upside
data.description:
Introduction

Texas Instruments (TXN) is the biggest producer of analog chips. With the recent announced intention to acquire Silicon Laboratories Inc. (SLAB), the latest annual report, and the Capital Management Presentation, which took place on 24th of February 2026, there is a lot of information that needs to be analyzed and evaluated. In this article, I want to describe in detail the capital allocation of TXN and look at the opportunities the company has in the future. The current higher share price already reflects in part the recovery in the analog chips market from my point of view. This is why I give this stock a hold rating and won’t add to my own position at this stage.

Q4 2025

On 27th January 2026, TXN reported fourth quarter results for the fiscal year 2025. Revenue was $4.4 billion and increased by 10% in comparison to Q4 2024. Diluted earnings per share (EPS) was $1.27 and decreased by 2% in comparison to Q4 2024. This decrease was mainly driven by higher cost of revenue, higher provision for income taxes, and lower other income. For fiscal year 2025 in total, revenue increased by 13%, from $15.6 billion to $17.7 billion. Diluted EPS increased by 5%, from $5.20 to $5.45. Cost of revenue increased disproportionately and is the main reason why the increase in revenue is not reflected in net income and diluted EPS.

During the conference call, the management gave some background information about the share of its end markets: in 2025, Industrial accounted for about 33% of revenue, Automotive was also 33%, and Data Center was 9%. In addition, Data Center grew by 64% in 2025 and is expected to continue strongly in 2026 which will be a driver for future revenue growth of TXN in my view. Industrial, Automotive, and Data Center, will be the end markets the company will focus on in the future. Personal electronics accounted for 21% of revenue, and communications equipment accounted for 3% of revenue.

By guiding for a revenue range of $4.3 billion to $4.7 billion for Q1 2026, the management seems optimistic about ongoing revenue increase. The midpoint of this guidance ($4.5 billion) would be an increase of about 10% in comparison to Q1 2025.

In 2025, the company spent $5.0 billion on dividends and $1.5 billion to buy back its own stock. Both numbers increased in comparison to 2024 ($4.8 billion in dividends and $0.9 billion in share repurchases). Free cash flows were $2.9 billion and $1.5 billion in 2025 and 2024, respectively, and well below the amount of money the company transferred to its shareholders.

Further analysis of the capital allocation of the company will now follow in the next section of the article.

Capital allocation

The company provides a capital management scorecard every year with its capital management presentation, which usually takes place in February. The latest capital management update was on 24th February 2026.

As you can see in the above picture, the goal with the highest priority is to maximize long-term growth of free cash flow per share. The company sees free cash flow per share growth as the one key performance indicator to maximize the share price over the long term.

Even the significant investments over the last couple of years, which led to a sharp reduction in free cash flow per share, will benefit the company in the long term, according to the management.

With a capital management scorecard, which is reviewed on a yearly basis, the management compares the goals that were set and what it has achieved.

As you can see in the picture, all but two targets were met. The free cash flow generation was below the targeted level of 25-35% of revenue due to the high capital expenditure in 2025. The dividend was increased by 4% despite the fact the dividend already consumed more than 80% of free cash flow—170%, to be precise. I expect both indicators to improve in 2026 and beyond due to lower capital expenditure and increasing revenue, which I will get to later in this article.

The current investment cycle is nearing its end

In 2021 the company started to invest more money to increase production capacity. Between the years of 2023 and 2025, capital expenditure amounted to between $4.5 and $5.1 billion.

Texas Instruments wants to increase the share of wafers it can produce internally, the share of 300mm wafers, as well as the share of internal assembly. To achieve this, the company is building additional production facilities in Lehi and Sherman. Simultaneously, the company will shut down its last two 150mm fabs in 2025 and 2026. The costs of the shutdowns were already included in the numbers for 2025.

Since the most capital-intensive part of the capacity expansion is completed, as you can see in the picture above, the company expects to decrease capital expenditure to $2 to $3 billion in 2026. Future capital expenditure will depend on revenue development and the need to add capacity. But since the company already finished the shell of its next production site (Sherman 2), it could add capacity rather quickly.

Free cash flow

As already mentioned, the highest goal of the company is to maximize free cash flow per share growth.

Between 2023 and 2025, free cash flow per share was very low and didn’t even cover the dividend payment. But starting in 2026, the company expects to increase free cash flow per share again meaningfully due to several reasons: first of all, the capital expenditure is expected to decrease to $2 to $3 billion in 2026, as already mentioned. All else equal, this adds about $1.6 to $2.6 billion to free cash flow. Secondly, the analog chips market is expected to continue its recovery, which would lead to higher revenue for TXN. In addition, the newly formed segment, Data Center, should contribute to future revenue growth as already described. Since TXN has low variable costs, any additional revenue leads to a high degree to higher free cash flow as well. Lastly, the described closure of the 150mm manufacturing sites will increase profitability and therefore free cash flow in comparison to previous years. Free cash flow was about $2.9 billion in 2025. The company expects free cash flow per share of at least $8 in 2026, which translates to a free cash flow of about $7.3 billion (assuming outstanding shares of 912 million). This amount of free cash flow would cover the dividend ($5.0-$5.4 billion) and would leave some room for share repurchases or debt reduction.

Acquisition of Silicon Laboratories

On the 4th of February, 2026, TXN announced its intention to acquire Silicon Laboratories for a total enterprise value of about $7.5 billion. With this acquisition, TXN wants to enhance its product portfolio of embedded wireless connectivity solutions. The parties expect that the deal will close in the first half of 2027. Since the $7.5 billion is a significant part of the free cash flow TXN is producing over the next couple of years, I put this acquisition in the section of capital allocation.

From my point of view, there are a couple of points that make this acquisition very interesting for TXN.

First of all, there are all the aspects the company is explaining in the presentation and the conference call that was held. It contributes to all four competitive advantages TXN has built: diversity and longevity of its products, reach of market channels, broad portfolio of analog and embedded products, and manufacturing and technology. The company expects annual synergies of at least $450 million, and the management didn’t include any expected revenue synergies in its calculation.

In addition, TXN expects to finance the transaction with cash on hand and debt. The additional leverage from this transaction should be neutralized within two years, according to a statement from the CFO during the conference call.

A very interesting part of this acquisition is the fact that Silicon Laboratories is a fabless chip company, which means it doesn’t have any manufacturing capacities and relies 100% on foundries for its chips to be produced. Since Texas Instruments has a very clean landscape when it comes to manufacturing sites with a high and increasing percentage of 300mm production, the company won’t face any integration costs regarding the combination of manufacturing sites. But since TXN has the capacity and know-how to produce at least 75% of the chips of Silicon Laboratories internally, it will move those 75%+ from external foundries to internal production. The remaining chips will be produced by external foundries due to the complexity of the chips. This move will improve the profitability of the chips of Silicon Laboratories significantly.

At the Bank of America Securities 2024 Global Technology Conference, the CFO Rafael Lazardi, mentioned that moving the production of embedded chips from external foundries to its own manufacturing in Lehi would decrease the variable costs per wafer from $2,500 to $200. Since then, the costs per wafer didn’t decrease, even for mature nodes like the ones Texas Instruments will need for the production of its own embedded chips but also for part of the portfolio Silicon Laboratories will bring in. By insourcing the production of the chips of Silicon Laboratories, TXN will make use of the capacity it has created. The aforementioned synergies of at least $450 million consist of about 60% of savings in terms of costs of goods sold (about $270 million). From my point of view, this cost reduction is related to the reduced variable cost per wafer I explained and is a conservative assumption.

Let us assume the costs of the production of a wafer are about 80% of all costs of revenues. In fiscal year 2025, Silicon Laboratories had a cost of revenues of $328 million. 80% of that $328 million would be about $262 million. The reduction of the cost per wafer from $2,500 to $200 would decrease those $262 million to about $21 million. The difference of about $240 million is already a large part of the $270 million in annual savings TXN tries to achieve when it comes to costs of goods sold. Any additional savings from assembly and testing can be added. If we assume revenue growth for Silicon Laboratories in 2026 and beyond, the anticipated savings would increase proportionally. This is why I think that the expected synergies are conservative and can be achieved.

Dividing the purchase price of $7.5 billion by the targeted synergies of $450 million brings us to a multiple of 16.7. Excluding one-time integration costs and temporary financing costs, the projected synergies are already justifying the deal for Texas Instruments, in my opinion. Higher annual synergies and potential revenue synergies will make this deal even more compelling. To sum it up, this intended acquisition is value accretive from my point of view and can lead to more revenue, profit, and free cash flow per share for Texas Instruments shareholders.

Dividends and Share buyback

An important part of the capital allocation strategy is payments to shareholders. Over time, Texas Instruments aims to distribute 100% of free cash flow to its owners.

As you can see in the picture, TXN has increased its dividends per share on a consistent basis and adds additional value to shareholders by repurchasing shares. From 2023 to 2025, repurchases were smaller than in previous years due to higher capital expenditures and the increased debt position the company has. In my point of view, the company can reaccelerate share repurchases after paying down some debt it has built due to the capital expenditures and after the payment of the acquisition of Silicon Laboratories.

Debt

Over the last couple of years, the company was committed to its increasing dividend policy despite higher capital expenditures. This led to an increase in debt. At the end of 2022, the company was cash neutral and increased the net debt position to about $9 billion at the end of 2025. Since capital expenditure will decrease in 2026 and beyond in comparison to previous years, TXN should be able to reduce net debt going forward if share repurchases remain modest. As mentioned in previous sections of this article, a surging free cash flow in 2026 and beyond should support dividend payments, the acquisition of Silicon Laboratories, and paying down debt. If the management were to decide to repurchase shares excessively, a reduction in net debt would be difficult to achieve, or it would lead to an increase in net debt. I would like to see a reduction in net debt back to a level close to cash neutral over the next couple of years. In a cyclical industry like chip manufacturing, a strong balance sheet is key to keeping the company out of trouble at all times.

Risks to the company

There are three main risks I want to highlight in this section:

The most obvious risk to the company and the strategy it is pursuing is a lack of revenue growth. If any crisis were to hit the world economy this or next year and revenue were to be lower than in 2025, the company would generate less profit and especially less free cash flow. The company still won’t be in trouble because it still has a solid balance sheet. But the costs of the capacity expansion would weigh on TXN and the increase in free cash flow would be further delayed.

Another risk that I need to mention is China and competitors from China. Up to now, the company sees an advantage to competitors from China due to their own low-cost production and the possibility to offer customers a wide range of different products. Most competitors are more specialized and can offer customers only a limited number of products. But if competitors from China improve their offerings and reduce their own costs, TXN might lose this advantage it currently has. In addition, the current dispute between the USA and China could lead to a ban of TXN products in China. Analog chips are currently less targeted than high-end chips (e.g., from Nvidia (NVDA)), but this could change in the future. The loss of all revenue from Chinese customers would be significant for TXN.

An additional risk arises from the intended acquisition of Silicon Laboratories. Higher than expected integration costs, lower than expected annual synergies, or any unforeseen problems would decrease the economic sense of the acquisition. This would cost Texas Instruments money and reputation and could lead to a decline in share price.

Valuation

The company currently has a forward-looking price-to-earnings ratio (P/E) of about 31. This compares to a P/E of about 29 for competitor Analog Devices (ADI) and about 25 for Infineon (IFNNY), which is another competitor. Even though P/E is expected to drop to 25 and 20 for fiscal years 2027 and 2028 respectively, I rate TXN only as a hold right now. The significant revenue and profit increases that are taken into account in those P/Es are yet to materialize. A lower starting point would be beneficial from my point of view for any future return. In addition, the company is currently valued above peers. Even though I think the company is superior in comparison to ADI and IFNNY (due to the discussed capital allocation and capacity expansion strategy), I will conservatively calculate with a similar valuation. Applying the estimated EPS for 2027 ($7.91) and a multiple of 23 (midpoint of P/E of ADI and IFNNY for 2027) leads to a share price of about $182, which is below the current price. Therefore, any significant drop below $180 seems like a good entry point to me.

One topic that could influence the stock price negatively is the pending acquisition of Silicon Laboratories. Even though the acquisition target is much smaller than TXN itself, a pending acquisition usually creates some uncertainty, which can weigh on the stock price until the acquisition is completed.

Summary

When it comes to the communication and execution of the capital allocation strategy, Texas Instruments is one of my favorite companies. It went through with its strategy to expand manufacturing capacity significantly, and it seems that the management made a good deal with the intended acquisition of Silicon Laboratories. In 2026 and beyond, the company can reap the benefits from its strategy. I am happy to add to my position in case of any unexpected significant drop in the share price, as long as the long-term thinking of the management stays the same.
